coredata.dc:title: Two-step hydrothermal synthesis of active carbon-dots with high temperature and salt resistance for injection pressure reduction and enhanced oil recovery
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lin
Sun, Yue
Wang, Zizhao
Liu, Jinming
Liu, Kaiyuanwen
Dai, Caili
Wu, Yining
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasmall carbon nanomaterials show enormous potential in the development of unconventional oil resources for reducing injection pressure and improving oil recovery. However, the high temperature and salinity reservoir conditions and the poor activity of carbon nanoparticles hinder their practical applications. In this study, carbon nanoparticles (CDs) are readily synthesized by citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) through a hydrothermal reaction. The results show that a change in the type and density of oxygen hydrophilic groups by adjusting the molar ratio of reactants can improve CDs dispersibility and stability at a temperature of 90 °C and salinity of 16 × 104 mg/L. The active carbon nanoparticle (sm-CDs) is obtained by surface modification of CDs with erucate amidopropyl betaine (EAB), which enhances surface activity while retaining the original temperature and salt resistance. Core flooding tests show 0.03 wt% sm-CDs nanofluid provides a pressure-reduced rate of 63.5% and an enhancement in oil recovery of 23.0%. The results of atomic force microscopy (AFM) with hydrophobic probe reveal sm-CDs can effectively reduce the adhesion between alkanes and lipophilic surfaces, which alter microscopic surface wetting from oil-wet to uniformly water-wet. This study provides a feasible strategy for the efficient development of carbon-based active nanofluids, especially in unconventional reservoirs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanoparticles
Hydrothermal reaction
Surface modification
Adhesion
Enhance oil recovery

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration for the synthesis of CDs and sm-CDs.
The physical (a) and XPS spectra (b) of CDs under different ratios of reactants.
Average diameter and zeta potential of CDs before and after 16 × 104 mg/L and 90 ℃ aging under different ratios of reactants.
TEM, HR-TEM (inset) images and corresponding statistics of particle distribution of CDs (a) and sm-CDs (b).

FT-IR spectra (a) and full range XPS spectra (b) of CDs and sm-CDs. High-resolution C 1 s (c), O 1 s(d),N 1 s (e) XPS spectra of CDs. High-resolution C 1 s (f), O 1 s(g),N 1 s (h) XPS spectra of sm-CDs.
IFT curves of simulated formation water, CDs and sm-CDs with different concentrations.
The contact angles of oil in (a) simulated formation water, (b) CDs and (c) sm-CDs.
Results of the injecting pressure (a) and recovery factor (b) during simulated formation water, CDs and sm-CDs nanofluid flooding.
Diagrams and frequency histograms of adhesion force between alkane and hydrophobic surface: (a) and (b) simulated formation water treated, (c) and (d) CDs treated, (e) and (f) sm-CDs.
Enhanced oil recovery and injection pressure reduction mechanism diagram of sm-CDs.
Particle size and zeta potential of CDs and sm-CDs before and after aging.

head.ce:title: Two-step hydrothermal synthesis of active carbon-dots with high temperature and salt resistance for injection pressure reduction and enhanced oil recovery

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shandong Key Laboratory of Oilfield Chemistry, Department of Petroleum Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of Oilfield Chemistry, Department of Petroleum Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasmall carbon nanomaterials show enormous potential in the development of unconventional oil resources for reducing injection pressure and improving oil recovery. However, the high temperature and salinity reservoir conditions and the poor activity of carbon nanoparticles hinder their practical applications. In this study, carbon nanoparticles (CDs) are readily synthesized by citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) through a hydrothermal reaction. The results show that a change in the type and density of oxygen hydrophilic groups by adjusting the molar ratio of reactants can improve CDs dispersibility and stability at a temperature of 90 °C and salinity of 16 × 104 mg/L. The active carbon nanoparticle (sm-CDs) is obtained by surface modification of CDs with erucate amidopropyl betaine (EAB), which enhances surface activity while retaining the original temperature and salt resistance. Core flooding tests show 0.03 wt% sm-CDs nanofluid provides a pressure-reduced rate of 63.5% and an enhancement in oil recovery of 23.0%. The results of atomic force microscopy (AFM) with hydrophobic probe reveal sm-CDs can effectively reduce the adhesion between alkanes and lipophilic surfaces, which alter microscopic surface wetting from oil-wet to uniformly water-wet. This study provides a feasible strategy for the efficient development of carbon-based active nanofluids, especially in unconventional reservoirs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the BP Statistic Review of Word Energy 2022, unconventional oil accounts for approximately 46.24% of the proportion of global oil resources and continues to increase [1]. In recent years, with continuous research on unconventional oil resources, they have become a hot spot in international petroleum exploration and development [2–4]. Unconventional reservoirs such as tight oil and shale oil mostly have micro/nano pores with low porosity and large heterogeneity [5,6]. The strong adhesion between crude oil and rock walls leads to difficulties associated with crude oil stripping and low washing efficiency [7,8]. In addition, the high reservoir temperature and high-salinity formation water of unconventional reservoirs poses strict requirements for displacement fluids [9,10]. Consequently, a new oil displacement agent is urgently needed.

Nanomaterials with small sizes and large specific surface areas show great advantages in regulating rock surface wettability and generating disjoining pressure [11,12]. Nanomaterials such as silica, titanium dioxide, graphene oxide and alumina have been proven to be effective in enhancing unconventional oilfield recovery [13–16]. However, ordinary nanomaterials with relatively large sizes (compared with nanoscale pore throats) easily block formations [17,18]. Poor high-temperature and high-salt resistance and low oil-water interfacial activity in the absence of other chemicals limit their practical application in unconventional reservoirs [19].

Ultrasmall (<10 nm) carbon-based nanomaterials have excellent dispersion stability, are easy to surface-modify and can be produced from a wide range of raw materials, showing great application potential in the development of tight unconventional reservoirs [20–23]. At present, the synthesis methods for carbon-based nanoparticles are mainly divided into top-down methods and bottom-up methods [24]. The hydrothermal method, as a bottom-up synthesis method with the greatest industrial application potential, has controllable reaction conditions, is simple to operate, and has high product yield and uniform product particle size [25,26]. Karpushkin et al. [27] used ascorbic acid as a raw material to roughly control the particle size of the synthesized product by controlling hydrothermal reaction time. Ioan-Alexandru Baragau et al. [28] used glucose and polysulfonate calixarene to synthesize zero-dimensional graphene carbon materials by a continuous hydrothermal method and evaluated their ability to reduce interfacial tension and regulate wettability to improve crude oil recovery. Kumar et al. [29]demonstrated that anionic surfactant could significantly improve the stability of silica nanofluid in saline environment and oil recovery. The abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of the carbon-based nanoparticles endow them with intrinsically excellent dispersion [30]. However, the strong hydrophilicity of conventional CDs limits the activity at oil-water interface, and the high interfacial tension of nano materials hinder the further improvement of oil recovery [31–33]. Traditional nanoparticle modification methods are difficult to simultaneously improve its temperature and salt resistance and oil-water interfacial activity, which restricts its practical application in unconventional oil fields.

In this study, carbon-based nanoparticle CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal reaction using citric acid and ethylenediamine as small molecular precursors. Increasing the type and density of oxygen hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs by adjusting the molar ratio of reactants can improve their temperature and salt resistance. sm-CDs was obtained by surface modification of CDs with EAB to enhance oil-water interfacial activity while retaining the original temperature and salt resistance of the CDs. Core flooding experiments show that the sm-CDs nanofluid has the ability to significantly reduce pressure and enhance oil recovery. In particular, from the perspective of nanomechanics, the microscopic displacement mechanism was investigated in detail and demonstrated via an AFM technique for the development of unconventional reservoirs.

Erucate amidopropyl betaine (EAB) with a purity above 40% was purchased from Huainan Huajun New Material Technology Co., Ltd. Citric acid monohydrate (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), sodium chloride, anhydrous calcium chloride, magnesium chloride hexahydrate and other inorganic salts were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dialysis bags (MD44, 1000 molecular weight) were purchased from Zhongke Yikang (Beijing) Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Cores with a permeability of 20 mD were purchased from Beijing Anmei Petroleum Scientific Research Instrument Co., Ltd. The crude oil used in the experiment was supplied by the Ta Yilian oilfield.

The synthetic routes for active carbon nanoparticles were shown in Fig. 1. CA and EDA with the same total mass (1.89 g) and different molar ratios (from 1:2, 2:3, 1:1, 3:2–2:1) were dispersed in 50 mL deionized water. They were placed in the polytetrafluoroethylene lining of the hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 12 h. The reaction products were filtered by a 0.1 µm filter and dialyzed by a 1000 D dialysis membrane for 72 h to remove large carbon impurities and residual small molecular precursors. Pure CDs nanoparticles were obtained by freeze-drying.

The CDs synthesized in the previous step were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water, and 0.5 g EAB was added (the optimization results of dosage of EAB were shown in the Table S1). The mixture was placed in the polytetrafluoroethylene lining of the hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 120 °C for 5 h. The same steps from the aforementioned reaction were repeated to obtain surfactant-modified sm-CDs nanoparticles.

The CDs synthesized with different molar ratios and the sm-CDs were characterized. The surface functional groups present on the CDs and sm-CDs were determined by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and recorded on a Nicolet IS10 spectrometer. The elements on the CDs and sm-CDs surfaces were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi) with a Mg Kα (1486.6 eV) monochromatic X-ray source.

The CDs synthesized with different molar ratios were dispersed in simulated formation water with a salinity of 16 × 104 mg/L ( Table 1) and kept at 90 °C for 7 days. The hydration particle size and zeta potential of CDs before and after ageing were measured by a dynamic light scattering particle size and zeta potential analyser (NanoBrook Omni, Brookhaven Instruments, U.S.A.). Finally, sm-CDs obtained by EAB modification of the CDs with the best dispersion stability were analysed for hydration particle size and zeta potential before and after ageing.

The interfacial tension of CDs and sm-CDs nanofluids at different concentrations (from 0.001 wt% to 0.1 wt%) of crude oil was measured by an interfacial rheometer (Tracker-S) and interfacial tension meter (TX-500 C, KRÜSS) at 90 °C. Quartz glass sheets were treated with 5 wt% dimethicone ethanol solution for 3 d at 110 °C to obtain an oleophilic substrate. The underwater oil contact angles of oleophilic glass sheets immersed in simulated formation water, CDs and sm-CDs nanofluids for 24 h were measured by contact angle meter (JC2000D2).

A gold-plated probe (NPG-10) was functionalized with 5 wt% N-dodecane thiol ethanol solution through Au-S bonds to fabricate the dodecane-functionalized hydrophobic probe [34]. The successful preparation of the hydrophobic probe was confirmed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7610 F) and energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS, S-4800 Hitachi), as shown in Fig. S1. In a 16 × 104 mg/L salinity aqueous environment, the interaction force between the hydrophobic probe and oleophilic mica surface before and after treatment with CDs and sm-CDs nanofluids was measured in force volume mode.

The oil displacement performance of the CDs and sm-CDs nanofluids prepared with 16 × 104 mg/L salinity water was evaluated using core flooding tests, as illustrated in Fig. 2. First, core physical parameters (diameter, length, porosity and permeability) were measured and recorded, as shown in Table 2. Then, the core was vacuumed for 12 h and saturated with simulated formation water to calculate the core pore volume. Next, the core was saturated with crude oil at a constant flow rate (0.02 mL/min) and aged at 90 °C for 7 d to establish natural wetting conditions.

Simulated formation water, 0.3 wt% CDs and 0.3 wt% sm-CDs were injected into the saturated core at a constant rate (0.1 mL/min) at 90 °C until the water content in the produced liquid exceeded 98%. Meanwhile, the displaced oil volume and flooding pressure were recorded.

The physical and XPS spectra of five CDs aqueous solutions synthesized with the same total mass and different reactant molar ratios were shown in Fig. 3. The colour saturation of the CDs aqueous solution was inversely proportional to the oxygen content of its surface groups. When the molar ratio of CA in the reactant reached 50%, the colour was the deepest, and the oxygen content was only 16.1%. When 30%, the colour was the lightest, and the oxygen content reached 19.5%.

The hydration particle size and zeta potential of the five CDs nanofluids before and after ageing were shown in Fig. 4. Before ageing, the hydration particle size of the five CDs nanofluids was less than 6 nm, and the absolute value of the zeta potential was greater than 25 mV. However, after ageing at 90 °C in 16 × 104 mg/L salinity water for 7 days, the hydration particle size and zeta potential of each CDs nanofluid increased to varying degrees. The hydration particle size of the CDs nanofluid synthesized with a CA molar ratio of 50% in the reactant only increased from 3.3 nm to 4.5 nm, and the absolute value of the zeta potential decreased from 33 mV to 30 mV. Therefore, a concentration of 50% CA was optimized. The infrared spectra and the approximate proportion of functional groups on the surface of CDs nanoparticles synthesized by different molar ratios of reactants were shown in Fig S2 and Table S2, respectively. With the increase of CA, the total proportion of high oxygen-containing groups (-COOH, -CONH2, -CO(C2H4NH2)H) showed a trend of decreasing first and then increasing. The carboxyl group was easy to react with other functional groups and cause agglomeration. Amide groups possessed limited temperature resistance. When the molar ratio of CA was 50%, amino and fewer high oxygen-containing groups endowed CDs with excellent temperature and salt resistance. Therefore, the CDs synthesized by 50% CA and 50% EDA was with the optimum stability. The results demonstrated the good stability of this CDs colloidal dispersion system, which resulted from the presence of a variety of hydrophilic groups. This CDs nanofluid showing satisfactory high-temperature resistance and high-salt tolerance was selected as the matrix for subsequent modification reactions.

The morphology of the CDs and sm-CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images. As shown in Fig. 5(a) and (b), CDs and sm-CDs were near spherical and monodispersed without any agglomeration. The HR-TEM images revealed CDs and sm-CDs both showed a clear lattice spacing of 0.11–0.12 nm (Fig. 5(a) and (b), insets), suggesting the high crystallinity of the samples. Statistical analysis demonstrated that the average particle size of sm-CDs was slightly larger than that of CDs, which were 1.65 ± 0.037 nm and 2.57 ± 0.010 nm, respectively. The slightly increase in particle size of sm-CDs should be attributed to the introduction of EAB.

FT-IR and XPS were used to measure the surface structure of CDs and sm-CDs, as shown in Fig. 6. For both spectra, the stretching vibrations of O-H, C-H, OC-H and C-N/CN peaks were observed near 3400 cm−1, 2900 cm−1, 1636 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1 in the FT-IR curve of the CDs and sm-CDs in Fig. 6(a). The surface composition and chemical state of CDs and sm-CDs were further analysed by XPS spectra. Compared with CDs, the content of C on the surface of the sm-CDs increased significantly (82.6%) due to the grafting of EAB long-chain alkanes, as shown in Fig. 6(b). The high-resolution C 1 s and N 1 s XPS spectra of CDs and sm-CDs (Fig. 6(c), (d), (e), (f)) can be subdivided into five characteristic peaks, corresponding to C-C (284.4–284.6 eV), C-N/C-O (285.5–286.0 eV), CO (287.4–287.8 eV), NH-(C)2 (399.9–401.3 eV) and C-N (399.2–399.6 eV). However, the N 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra of sm-CDs contained an additional N(C)4+ (402.0 eV) characteristic peak, indicating the successful grafting of EAB. Combined with the FT-IR results, it can be verified that there were -OH, -CONH2, -NH2, and -CON(C2H4NH2)H hydrophilic groups on the surface of the CDs and sm-CDs. The proportion of surface functional groups of CDs and sm-CDs was roughly calculated by fitting the peak areas of the XPS spectrum, and the error was less than 10% [35,36]. After surface modification, the ratio of hydrophilic groups decreased. The percentage of -CON(C2H4NH2)H decreased from 22% to 18%, that of -CONH2 decreased from 21% to 16%, that of -NH2 decreased from 51% to 31% and that of -OH decreased from 6% to 3%. The large decrease in the ratio of -NH2, -CON(C2H4NH2)H and -OH was due to the dehydration condensation reaction between the hydrophilic head -COOH of EAB and the other groups during the modification process. Eventually, the introduced hydrophobic alkane chains accounted for 32% on the surface of the sm-CDs.

The CDs and sm-CDs nanofluids were aged at 90 °C in 16 × 104 mg/L salt water for 7 days, and their hydration particle size and zeta potential were measured as shown in Table 3. The hydration particle size and zeta potential of sm-CDs did not change significantly after ageing. The hydration particle size increased from 6.5 nm to 8.3 nm, and the absolute value of the zeta potential decreased from 31 mV to 28 mV. Compared with CDs, the size and stability of sm-CDs have not changed greatly after modification. The abundant hydrophilic groups (oxygen-containing groups and amino) on the surface of CDs and sm-CDs greatly promoted their dispersion stability in high-salinity brine [37]. In contrast, CDs and sm-CDs did not contain groups such as -COOH that easily reacted with other groups, resisting high temperatures to a certain extent.

Reducing oil-water interfacial tension (IFT) and regulating the wettability of oil-wet rock surfaces are important for improving oil recovery. The stable values of IFT of CDs and sm-CDs at a concentration of 0.001 wt%∼0.1 wt% were shown in Fig. 7. It could be seen that the oil-water interfacial tension value of sm-CDs did not simply decrease by the increasing of concentration, but owned an optimal concentration. When exceeded this value, the interfacial tension would tend to be stable or increase slightly [38,39]. The interfacial tension of sm-CDs was one to two orders of magnitude lower than that of CDs. The lowest interfacial tension 0.06 mN/m (less than 0.1 mN/m) for sm-CDs was achieved when the concentration was 0.03 wt%, which was considered the optimum concentration for follow-up research. Meanwhile, in order to prevent the deviation of oil displacement performance caused by different mass concentrations, the concentration of CDs was also optimized to be 0.03 wt%. An important reason for the lower interfacial tension between sm-CDs and crude oil was that there were not only hydrophilic groups (-OH, -CONH2, -NH2, -CON(C2H4NH2)H) on the surface of sm-CDs but also grafted lipophilic tail chains of EAB. This presented the possibility for sm-CDs to diffuse to the oil-water interface. The amphiphilic sm-CDs can achieve hydrophilic-lipophilic balance (HLB) at the oil-water interface to reduce interfacial tension [40].

To explore the ability of CDs and sm-CDs to change the wettability of the lipophilic surface, the underwater oil contact angle was measured, as shown in Fig. 8. After oleophilic treatment, the underwater oil contact angle on the glass slide was 35°, indicating that the surface was modified to an oil-wet state. CDs could only change the underwater contact angle to 99°, but sm-CDs could change it to 120° in 48 h. The results showed that CDs can only convert the hydrophobic surface into an intermediate wetting state, and sm-CDs can induce water-wetting. An important reason for this excellent wetting performance was that the hydrophobic tails of sm-CDs are hydrophobically associated with lipophilic components on the surface, exposing the hydrophilic head group (-CONH2, -NH2, etc.) to achieve wettability reversal [33]. On the other hand, due to their ultrasmall nanostructures (6.5 nm) and good interfacial activity (0.06 mN/m), the sm-CDs could form a self-assembled two-dimensional layered structure in the three-phase area (crude oil/ sm-CDs nanofluid/ lipophilic surface), generating a separation pressure perpendicular to the oil-water interface that is higher than that of the nanosolution [41,42]. The separation pressure induced the sm-CDs nanofluid to move along the direction of mutual peeling between the oil droplets and rock surface, which was beneficial to oil displacement.

Based on the previous experimental data, 0.03 wt% CDs and sm-CDs, which showed excellent performances in reducing IFT and modifying surface wettability, were evaluated for oil-displacement and pressure-reduction performance. The variation trends in the injection pressure and the recovery factor were recorded in Fig. 9. Under the same experimental conditions, the injection pressure increased sharply to 575 kPa in the first 1.4 PV and decreased rapidly after the breakthrough capillary force during the simulated formation water flooding process. The injection pressure stabilized at 380 kPa when 4.8 PV water was injected, with no oil emerging at the outlet. The final oil recovery rate was calculated to be 46.8%. In the process of CDs and sm-CDs nanofluid flooding, the injection pressure showed the same trend, rising constantly in the initial stage, followed by a gentle decline. The injection pressure of the CDs and sm-CDs nanofluids stabilized at 330 kPa and 210 kPa, affording oil recovery factors of 57.5% and 69.8%, respectively.

Therefore, compared with water and CDs nanofluid flooding, the sm-CDs nanofluid can more efficiently reduce the injection pressure and increase oil recovery, with superior pressure-reducing rates of 63.5% and 36.4% and enhanced oil recoveries of 23.0% and 12.3%, respectively. This was because on the one hand, the sm-CDs nanofluid was more strongly adsorbed to the rock wall, making the rock surface hydrophilic and reducing turbulence [43]. On the other hand, the low interfacial tension between the oil and water made the nanoparticles diffuse to the oil-water interface to form an interfacial film, causing the oil droplets to break into ultrasmall sizes more easily and be carried out.

AFM was used to determine the interaction between the hydrophobic surface and alkanes before and after the adsorption of CDs and sm-CDs to clarify the mechanism of enhancement. According to extended DLVO theory, the adhesion force of colloidal particles includes the van der Waals force (vdW), electric double layer interactions (EDLs) and possible hydrophilic/hydrophobic forces [44,45]. In this experiment, the values of van der Waals force (vdW) and the electric double layer interaction (EDL) did not change significantly before and after modification due to the unchanged test environment and the shielding of salt ions, so the change of adhesion force could be corresponded to the change of hydrophilic/hydrophobic force [46]. The adhesion force ( Fig. 10 (a), (c), and (e)) and frequency distribution histograms (Fig. 10 (b), (d), and (f)) before and after adsorption were as follows.

The colour in the adhesion force diagrams from light to dark indicate that the hydrophobic force decreased and hydrophilicity increased gradually. After CDs and sm-CDs treatment, the colour of the lipophilic mica became darker, and the colour was darkest after sm-CDs treatment (Fig. 10 (e)). Generally, the value corresponding to the peak value of the fitting curve in the frequency histogram is used to represent the average adhesion force [47]. In the corresponding force frequency distribution histogram (Fig. 10 (f)), the value of the average adhesion force after sm-CDs nanofluid treatment was only 19.4 pN. However, the adhesion forces between the lipophilic mica surfaces treated by simulated formation water (Fig. 10 (b)) and CDs nanofluid (Fig. 10 (d)) and the alkane chains were 1.66 nN and 143.7 pN, respectively. The results indicated that sm-CDs induce the greatest change in wettability, which was consistent with the previous contact angle experimental results. Moreover, it can be seen that the lipophilic surface is more homogeneous from the obvious narrowing of the distribution range of sm-CDs adhesion in the force frequency histogram. The significantly reduced adhesion energy of oil droplets on the lipophilic surface and the modification of the lipophilic surface to achieve uniform wetting allowed the oil droplets to be stripped more easily [28].

The above results demonstrated that the sm-CDs nanofluid at an optimized concentration of 0.03 wt% can effectively reduce the injection pressure and enhance oil recovery. The abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface endowed sm-CDs with superior dispersion stability and high salt resistance. Additionally, the grafting of EAB long-chain alkanes effectively enhanced the oil-water and oil-solid interfacial activity. As shown in Fig. 11, compared with water and CDs nanofluid flooding, sm-CDs nanofluid possessed more excellent ability to enhance oil recovery. When the sm-CDs nanofluid was injected into the core, sm-CDs could adsorb to the rock and change the wettability of the surface from oil-wet to water-wet, making it easier for oil droplets to desorb from the solid surface owing to the structural separation pressure of the wedge-shaped film. The formation of low interfacial tension overcomes the Jamin effect, so that the peeled oil droplets can be successfully displaced through the nanoscale pore throat. The hydrophilic surface could also significantly reduce the adhesion force between alkanes and rock, which is conducive to oil droplet stripping. In summary, these versatile interactions of the sm-CDs nanofluid jointly achieve injection pressure reduction and enhanced oil recovery.

Active nanoparticle sm-CDs was successfully synthesized by a two-step hydrothermal method. The increase in the type and density of oxygen hydrophilic groups (-OH, -CONH2, -NH2, -CON(C2H4NH2)H) endowed the sm-CDs with superior dispersion stability and high temperature and salt (90 ℃, 16 ×104 mg/L salinity water) resistance. The grafting of EAB long-chain alkanes effectively improved the oil-water and oil-solid interfacial activity, which reduced the interfacial tension from 17.02 mN/m to 0.06 mN/m and the underwater contact angle from 99° to 120°. Moreover, the adhesion between alkanes and rock was notably reduced to 19.4 pN. Compared with water and CDs nanofluid flooding, the sm-CDs nanofluid efficiently reduced the injection pressure and increased oil recovery, with superior pressure-reducing rates of 63.5% and 36.4% and enhanced oil recoveries of 23.0% and 12.3%, respectively. This work provides a novel oil displacement agent for the harsh reservoir environments of unconventional oil fields.

Here is an overview of the individual contributions of all authors to this study. Lin Li: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yue Sun: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Zizhao Wang: Data curation. Jinming Liu: Formal analysis. Kaiyuanwen Liu: Methodology. Caili Dai: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yining Wu: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study is financially supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2019YFA0708700), National Natural Science Foundation of China (52174054).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.132115.

.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Global progress in exploration and development of unconvetional hydrocarbons and assessment of resources potential
Mar. Geol. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wu, Z. Sun, T. Yang, S. Wang, W. Zhang, Global progress in exploration and development of unconvetional hydrocarbons and assessment of resources potential, Marine Geology Letters, 36 (2020) 1–17.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Resource recovery and reuse for hydraulic fracturing wastewater in unconventional shale gas and oil extraction
Environ. Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Chang, B. Liu, J.C. Crittenden, R.D. Vidic, Resource recovery and reuse for hydraulic fracturing wastewater in unconventional shale gas and oil extraction, Environmental Science & Technology, 53 (2019) 13547–13548.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Wettability alteration and spontaneous imbibition in unconventional liquid reservoirs by surfactant additives
SPE Reserv. Eval. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.O. Alvarez, D.S. Schechter, Wettability alteration and spontaneous imbibition in unconventional liquid reservoirs by surfactant additives, SPE Reservoir Evaluation & Engineering, 20 (2017) 107–117.
